Tafseer:
1. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth glorifies Allah, the Sovereign, the Pure, the All-Mighty, the All-Wise) (1).
(Introduction): This introductory verse has appeared in previous chapters with minor variations in wording. In the previous Surah, the past tense form 'sabbaha' [or 'yusabbih'] was used, which depicts the present state. Here, four attributes of Allah Almighty are mentioned: 'Al-Malik', which means the King/Sovereign; 'Al-Quddus', which means pure from every defect and blemish; 'Al-Aziz', which—as has been repeatedly clarified—means the Dominant and Mighty; and 'Al-Hakim', the Being in whose every word and deed there is wisdom. These four attributes appear here as an introduction to the subsequent verse. Their explanation is best suited under the commentary of that verse.

(He is the One Who raised among the unlettered a messenger from among themselves, reciting to them His verses, purifying them, and teaching them the Book and wisdom, although they were previously in manifest error) (2). It is stated that the same God, Who is the true King of this universe, raised a messenger from among the unlettered people themselves so that he might recite His verses to them, purify them, and teach them the Book and wisdom. If you ponder, you will realize that the attributes of Allah Almighty listed in the introductory verse have been put into effect through the sending of this Messenger mentioned here.
(The mission of the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was for the fulfillment of the requirements of Allah's attributes): He is the true King of creation. The requirement of this attribute of His is that He sent His Messenger to mankind to inform them of His commands and guidance, whose description is: (reciting to them His verses) he is reciting His teachings and guidance to the people. He is Pure and Holy (Al-Quddus), hence He willed to purify people through His Messenger and His teachings; thus, His Messenger is purifying them from the evils of beliefs, actions, and character (and purifies them).
Furthermore, He is All-Mighty and All-Wise (Al-Aziz, Al-Hakim), which is why He sent such a Messenger who is teaching His servants the Shariah and wisdom. Here, the word 'Book' is used in the sense of Shariah and law. The effective implementation of Shariah and law comes from the one who is dominant and powerful, but Allah Almighty is not just dominant and powerful, He is also All-Wise; for this reason, the law He is teaching through His Messenger is not merely a manifestation of His power, but also a manifestation of His wisdom and the worldly and otherworldly welfare of His servants. This verse is in the context of bestowing favor and grace upon the Children of Ishmael, which is why the word 'Ummiyyin' (unlettered) is used here as a distinguishing description for them. This term has already been discussed in its relevant place, but it is necessary to recall here that although this terminology was coined by the People of the Book, particularly the Jews, reflecting their religious pride and disdain for the Arabs, the Children of Ishmael, being unfamiliar with the Book and Shariah, accepted this title for themselves without any feeling of inferiority. Then, when the Qur'an mentioned this word for them and for the Messenger sent to them as a distinguishing characteristic, its status became so elevated that it became like a heavenly honor for the Arabs. Thus, the glory of divine power was manifested: those who were demeaned as unlettered and uncivilized were appointed to teach and civilize the entire world, while those who took pride in possessing the Book and Shariah were declared to be like 'a donkey carrying books'.
Here, this word is used to evoke a sense of gratitude in the Arabs, reminding them to be grateful to their Lord for showing mercy to them, raising a Messenger from among themselves to reform, train, and bless them with the Book and wisdom, and bringing them out of the darkness of ignorance in which they had been trapped due to their unlettered state. The point is that those who have received this blessing should cherish it and ensure that others appreciate it too, lest they be deprived of it due to ungratefulness, thus fulfilling the desire of the envious. We have discussed the attributes of the Unlettered Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) mentioned here in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. You may refer to it to dispel the misconceptions spread by the rejectors of Hadith regarding the purposes of the Prophet's mission. Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him) had prayed for a prophet with these exact attributes to be raised among the Children of Ishmael. In Surah Al-Baqarah, this prayer is mentioned as:
(Our Lord, and send among them a messenger from themselves who will recite to them Your verses and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them. Indeed, You are the Exalted in Might, the Wise.)
(The Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is the manifestation of Abraham's prayer): This shows that just as this Prophet is a manifestation of Allah Almighty's attributes, he is also the manifestation of the prayer of Abraham (peace be upon him) which he made for the progeny of Ishmael. Thus, diverse qualities are combined in him: he is the glad tidings of Jesus and the prayer of Abraham, the manifestation of Allah's attributes, and furthermore, he is an individual from among yourselves. There is no veil of strangeness or foreignness between you and him. Those who look down on you cannot taunt you that you received light through them or anyone else; rather, Allah elevated you above all creation by making you the means to illuminate the entire world through him.
(The mission of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is to all creation): It should be clear here that although the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was raised among the unlettered, his call is to all creation. We have discussed this issue in detail in its relevant place. Here, only remember that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was sent with two missions: a specific mission and a general mission. His specific mission was to the Children of Ishmael, and the duties of this mission were completed by the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself. His general mission, which is to all creation, was entrusted to his Ummah, who were honored with the position of 'witnesses of Allah on earth' to perform these duties until the Day of Resurrection. Since the unlettered (Arabs) formed the vanguard of the 'witnesses of Allah on earth', it is not incorrect to say that Allah showed light to all creation through those who were unlettered.
(And although they were previously in manifest error): This is to draw attention to the darkness that hung over them in the past, in order to evoke a sense of gratitude in the unlettered. The point is that if they reflect on the pitch-black darkness of ignorance in which they were trapped, only then will they realize the scale of their Lord's grace and favor, seeing from what pit of darkness He extracted them and to what heights of honor and dignity He raised them.

(And [to] others of them who have not yet joined them. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise) (3).
(A powerful and subtle invitation to those unlettered ones who were still deprived of Islam): This is linked to the 'unlettered' (Ummiyyin) mentioned earlier. That is, among the unlettered people in whom this Messenger was raised, there are others who have not yet joined them. This refers to those Children of Ishmael who had not yet accepted Islam. This implies that although the word 'unlettered' was used generally above, it specifically referred to those Children of Ishmael who had already embraced Islam. Indeed, the final part of the verse (And although they were previously in manifest error) serves as proof of this. Now, in this verse, in a very beautiful and eloquent style, an invitation is extended to those Children of Ishmael who were still distant from Islam. The word 'minhum' (from them) indicates that among these unlettered people for whom this heavenly blessing descended, there are others who are still reluctant. They are invited in an exceptionally subtle manner, suggesting that it is a pity if those for whom this feast of blessings is spread, and who are the first to be invited, should remain deprived of it. The words 'lama yalhaqu bihim' (they have not yet joined them) also contain a glad tiding that although they have not joined yet, they will do so soon. This is a prophecy of their acceptance of Islam in the near future, similar to the glad tiding in Surah Al-Mumtahanah, verse 7.
(Divine practice regarding guidance): (And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise) - Here, Allah Almighty points to His established way regarding faith and guidance. If He willed, He could guide all of creation, for He is All-Mighty and Dominant. However, He is also All-Wise, so He only blesses with guidance those who deserve it according to His wisdom. In Surah Ad-Dahr, this truth is expressed as: (And you do not will except that Allah wills. Indeed, Allah is ever Knowing and Wise. He admits whom He wills into His mercy) (Ad-Dahr, 76:30-31).

(That is the favor of Allah, which He bestows upon whom He wills, and Allah is the possessor of great bounty) (4).
(Rebuttal of Jewish envy): This refers to the great favor that Allah Almighty bestowed upon the unlettered, as mentioned above. It is stated that this is Allah's favor; no one has a monopoly over it. He chooses whom He wills for it, and His choosing is based on His own wisdom. No one else has any say in it. The point is that if the Jews are envious of this, they can be as envious as they want. In doing so, they will only harm themselves and cannot harm anyone else. God gave the unlettered what belonged to Him, not what belonged to anyone else, so there is no basis for their anger.

(The example of those who were entrusted with the Torah and then did not take it up is like that of a donkey who carries volumes [of books]. Wretched is the example of the people who deny the signs of Allah. And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people) (5).
(A blow to Jewish pride): This deals a blow to the pride of the Jews, who believed that they were the only ones worthy of holding the Book and Shariah, and that no one else could rival them in this honor. They must discard this arrogance from their minds. Now, their example is like that of a donkey carrying a load of books but having no idea what is written in them. (They were entrusted with the Torah and then did not take it up) - that is, there is no doubt that at one time the burden of the Torah was placed upon them, but it is also a reality that they did not truly carry this heavy burden. This failure to carry it is explained by the words (they denied the signs of Allah), meaning they no longer believed in the teachings and commandments of the Torah and practically rejected them. Obviously, when they rejected the commands of these books, they were deprived of their reward; only the burden of guilt remained on their heads, fitting the description of 'an animal carrying books'.
In the phrase 'hammulu' (they were entrusted), the word 'hammulu' is highly eloquent. It suggests that the Torah, whose possession they boast of today, was not accepted by them with eagerness and desire when it was given to them; rather, it was forced upon them. The full details of this enforcement have been mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah, showing how the Jews demonstrated stubbornness and defiance in accepting every single command of the Torah, and how Prophet Moses (peace be upon him) lamented their state with great sorrow. A brief reference to this is also found in Surah As-Saff, verse 5. (Wretched is the example of the people who deny the signs of Allah) - It is stated that those whose example is so unsightly have no right to indulge in delusions of sanctity and closeness to God, thinking that God cannot make any other nation the bearer of the Book and Shariah while they exist.
(One who does not act upon the Book is not its true bearer): The words 'they denied the signs of Allah' clarify the preceding phrase 'then they did not take it up'. Failing to carry the Torah means that they rejected its commands through their actions. They claim with their tongues to be its bearers, but when they do not act upon it, how can they be considered its true bearers?
(And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people): This phrase also appeared in Surah As-Saff, verse 7. It points to the fact that because of their evil deeds, they have been cursed, and Allah has sealed their hearts, as detailed in Surah Al-Baqarah. Therefore, they are not going to receive guidance. They have wronged their own souls. Allah did not wrong them; they were given the Torah but they rejected it, and now, under the illusion of being the bearers of that very Torah, they are rejecting the final Messenger of Allah, questioning how a messenger could be raised in any other nation besides theirs. It is stated that God does not guide such unfortunate people. People of this sort will always continue to stumble.

(Say, 'O you who are Jews, if you claim that you are allies of Allah, excluding the [other] people, then wish for death, if you should be truthful') (6).
(Another blow to Jewish pride): This deals another blow to their pride. If they claim to be the beloved of God, then the requirement of this love and belovedness is that they should have the desire and passion to give their lives in the way of God. A lover's greatest desire is to meet the beloved, and they do not run away from what opens the path to meeting the beloved. Yet, your state is such that no one is more cowardly than you when facing death. Refer to the commentary on their condition in Surah As-Saff, verses 4 and 5. In Surah Al-Hashr, the cowardice of Banu Qurayzah and their allies is depicted in these words:
(You are more feared in their breasts than Allah. That is because they are a people who do not understand. They will not fight you all except within fortified cities or from behind walls. Their violence among themselves is severe. You think they are together, but their hearts are diverse. That is because they are a people who do not reason.) (Al-Hashr, 59:13-14).
In Surah Al-Baqarah, this same subject is described in more detail as follows:
(Say, 'If the home of the Hereafter with Allah is for you peculiarly, excluding the [other] people, then wish for death, if you should be truthful.' And they will never wish for it, ever, because of what their hands have sent before them. And Allah is Knowing of the wrongdoers. And you will surely find them the most greedy of people for life—even more than those who associate partners with Allah. One of them wishes if he could be granted a life of a thousand years, but it would not remove him in the least from the punishment that he should be granted life. And Allah is Seeing of what they do.) (Al-Baqarah, 2:94-96).
(The phrase 'excluding the people' refers to the Children of Ishmael): Although the words 'excluding the people' (min dun in-nas) in the verse under discussion are general, the reference here is specifically to the Children of Ishmael. Due to the prophecies of the Torah and ethnic rivalry, the Jews harbored the greatest animosity toward them. The complete history of this animosity has been detailed in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. This animosity existed from the beginning, but when the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was sent and they realized that the anticipated event had occurred, this fire of envy was fully ignited.
(The entire history of the Jews bears witness to their cowardice): The Qur'an here mocks the cowardice of the Jews. Although they could have shamelessly claimed in response that they did not fear death, a person's inner self is not hidden from them. They felt that the Qur'an had touched their most sensitive nerve, so they chose to remain silent. How could they deny something to which both their past history and current events bore witness?

(But they will never wish for it, ever, because of what their hands have sent before them. And Allah is Knowing of the wrongdoers) (7).
This points to the cause of their cowardice and their extreme fear of death: their own deeds. They are the killers of prophets, they distorted the Torah, they betrayed Allah's trusts, and they erased all the signs of guidance they were commanded to clarify to mankind. With what face will they present themselves before their Lord? Nevertheless, they must return to Him, and He is fully aware of the wrongdoers. He will fully recompense everyone for what they have done.

(Say, 'Indeed, the death from which you flee—indeed, it will meet you. Then you will be returned to the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, and He will inform you about what you used to do') (8).
The warning implied in the preceding phrase (And Allah is Knowing of the wrongdoers) is fully detailed in this verse. It is stated: Flee from death if you must, but where will you go to escape it? It will surely overtake you. Then, you will be brought with all your evil deeds before your God, Who knows all that is hidden and manifest. Neither is any of your actions hidden from Him, nor can you conceal anything from Him. He will lay bare your entire record before you, and you will have to suffer the punishment for every crime.

2. Subject Matter of Verses 9-11
Next, the Muslims are admonished for a mistake committed by a group regarding the Friday prayer and respect for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). A trade caravan entered Medina, and upon hearing the news, some people left the mosque at the exact time the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was delivering the sermon (Khutbah). Although this act was committed by a few newly disciplined individuals, it highlighted certain weaknesses in the community that needed correction.
One weakness was that many had not yet developed a proper appreciation of the great favor Allah Almighty bestowed upon the unlettered by sending the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Second, people had not yet fully understood the reality of the transaction mentioned in Surah As-Saff, verses 10-13—that those who pledge listening and obedience to the Messenger sell their lives and wealth to Allah in exchange for His forgiveness and Paradise. It is not permissible for them to prioritize any other trade to the extent of leaving Allah's Messenger standing while delivering the sermon to head out to the market.
Third, it became clear that people had not yet properly appreciated the sanctity of Friday. This day is for Muslims what the Sabbath was for the Children of Israel. When the Children of Israel compromised the sanctity of the Sabbath due to worldly greed and the desire for hunting, Allah cursed them and transformed their appearances. If Muslims compromise the sanctity of Friday out of greed for trade, there is no reason they should remain secure from Allah's anger.
These matters are of special importance, as they represent a clear disregard for the great favor that Allah, contrary to the desires of the Jews, bestowed upon the unlettered, and which is emphasized in this Surah. For this reason, those who committed these acts were admonished. Read the verses in this light.
3. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(O you who have believed, when the call is made for the prayer on the day of Jumu'ah [Friday], then hasten to the remembrance of Allah and leave business. That is better for you, if you only knew) (9).
(A wise style of address): Although the address is general, it is directed toward those whose weakness is being corrected. The benefit of a general address is that those whose behavior is under discussion do not feel overly humiliated; if they possess soundness of heart, they perceive this discretion as a sign of the speaker's generosity and benefit from the advice. If they were singled out by name, it might evoke stubbornness and pride.
(The Friday Adhan): In 'for the prayer on the day of Jumu'ah', the words 'on the day of Jumu'ah' clarify the prayer so it is clear that the Friday prayer is specifically under discussion. The Friday prayer is the sole element that distinguishes Friday from other days. The call to prayer mentioned here refers to the Adhan delivered before the Friday sermon. It is established from undisputed narrations that during the era of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the first two Caliphs, there was only one Adhan for Friday, which was given before the sermon. Caliph Uthman added another Adhan during his caliphate when the population of Medina increased. This Adhan was delivered from his house in the market of Medina. Although this Adhan was an addition by Uthman, it was accepted by the righteous of the Ummah without objection.
(Then hasten to the remembrance of Allah): The word 'sa'y' (hastening) does not merely mean running; rather, it refers to performing a task with promptness, energy, and dedication. When a servant quickly responds to his master's call, this is 'sa'y'. It is commanded: When you hear this Adhan, hasten to the remembrance of Allah and abandon buying and selling.
(The Sermon and the Friday Prayer): 'The remembrance of Allah' (dhikrillah) here is a comprehensive term for both the sermon and the prayer. While using 'dhikr' for prayer is clear—as the Qur'an frequently refers to prayer as remembrance—the Friday sermon is also essentially a part of the prayer. The Friday prayer stands in place of the Dhuhr prayer; its four units (rak'ahs) are reduced to two on Friday, and the sermon takes the place of the remaining two units as a form of remembering Allah. The Imam delivers this remembrance to those present in the mosque. Therefore, it encompasses all matters relevant to the reform and welfare of Muslims as dictated by current circumstances. There is no reason to restrict it to a few formal supplications.
(Care and Commitment for the Sermon and Prayer): 'And leave business' (wa dharu'l-bay') - the word 'bay'' literally means selling, but in common usage, it refers to both buying and selling. Although only abandoning 'selling' is mentioned—which is relatively more desirable—abandoning 'buying' is required to an even greater degree. The objective is to state that every worldly affair should be left aside to hasten toward prayer. Business is specifically mentioned only because the incident that tested the Muslims and is being corrected here was related to trade.
(That is better for you, if you only knew): That is, outwardly this command may seem difficult to some of you, and you may perceive a loss in your business, but in reality, this practice is a source of goodness and blessing for you. Sustenance and favor are entirely in Allah's hands. If you tolerate a temporary loss for His pleasure, you will secure its reward with your Lord in the eternal life, and He may also create circumstances in this world to compensate for your losses. (If you only knew) - meaning you should not calculate your profit and loss based solely on the limited life of this world, but should also keep the Hereafter in view. However, the Hereafter is something you will only understand if you make an effort to understand it.

(And when the prayer has been concluded, disperse within the land and seek from the bounty of Allah, and remember Allah often that you may succeed) (10).
(In contrast to the Jewish Sabbath, the Friday restriction is easy to observe): That is, this restriction is only for the duration of the prayer. Once you are finished, you are permitted to go wherever you wish and seek Allah's sustenance and favor in whatever way you choose. The point is that if you cannot tolerate even such a brief restriction for the pleasure of your Lord, then what is the value of your faith and Islam? It should also be kept in mind that the restriction of the Sabbath for the Jews lasted the entire day, whereas for this Ummah, the restriction of Friday is only from the Adhan until the end of the prayer. During the rest of the day, there is the same freedom as on other days.
(And remember Allah often that you may succeed): That is, there is no restriction on your efforts to seek sustenance, but if you do not want this world to be your sole focus and desire success in the Hereafter as well, the way to achieve this is to remember Allah at every step. This remembrance will protect you from the deceptions and trials of Satan; otherwise, Satan will blind you to the point where you lose the distinction between lawful and unlawful, becoming obsessed with this world, and ultimately ending up as fuel for Hell.

(But when they saw a transaction or some amusement, they dispersed to it and left you standing. Say, 'What is with Allah is better than amusement and than transaction, and Allah is the best of providers') (11).
(The incident that occasioned this admonition): Finally, reference is made to the incident that led to these admonitions and teachings. Narrations indicate that a trade caravan entered Medina at the exact time of the Friday sermon. Following the custom of the time, they beat drums to announce their arrival, prompting some people to leave the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) standing during the sermon and rush toward the caravan. Such caravans held great importance in those days, as the buying and selling of essential items depended on them. People eagerly awaited them, and upon their arrival, everyone tried to outdo one another to secure needed goods and sell their own merchandise. Those who committed this act were clearly not yet fully refined by Islamic training, and they seemed unaware of the true importance of the Friday sermon. For them, the primary importance lay only in the prayer itself; they likely assumed they could check the caravan and return before the prayer started. Regardless, their mistake resulted in the revelation of instructions regarding Friday, the Friday sermon, and the status of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that had not been revealed before.
Although the verse uses a general form of address, it is clear that this act was committed only by a few untrained individuals. The general style of Qur'anic admonition is to correct using broad terms rather than naming individuals, ensuring that everyone in the community can benefit without causing humiliation to any specific group. The words (and left you standing) highlight the gravity of the incident—that the sermon was being delivered by the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself. Leaving the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in this manner carries implications of disrespect and a disregard for religion. This is similar to the attitude the Jews showed toward Prophet Moses (peace be upon him), which resulted in Allah turning their hearts away, as described in Surah As-Saff. Therefore, the Qur'an corrected them immediately.
(Say, 'What is with Allah is better than amusement and than transaction, and Allah is the best of providers'): It is stated: Seek the great reward that Allah has in store for the sincere believers. It is far better than the amusement and trade of this world. Even if you suffer a massive worldly loss to gain the pleasure of God and His Messenger, you will trade worthless pebbles for an eternal kingdom. Conversely, if you displease God and His Messenger to accumulate all the wealth of the world, how long will it last? Wisdom dictates that you should not chase after this world, but seek what is with Allah. Allah is the best of providers; He provides sustenance from sources unimaginable and grants sustenance that is honorable in every respect.
The linguistic styles of 'la'allakum tuflihun' and 'Khayr ur-Raziqin' have been explained in their respective places.
4. Points of Guidance from the Qur'an Regarding Friday, the Friday Sermon, and the Status of Prophethood
While we have highlighted important points in the commentary of the verses, a few additional matters merit attention:
(The Prophet's explanation is Allah's teaching): First, the instructions given to Muslims regarding the Friday prayer, its Adhan, and its sermon, along with the correction of their mistake, indicate that all matters related to establishing Friday were carried out by the command of Allah Almighty. This is despite the fact that Friday is not mentioned anywhere else in the Qur'an before or after this. It is established from narrations that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) organized it after migrating to Medina and taught the people its rules and etiquettes. When some people fell short in observing these etiquettes, the Qur'an corrected them as if they had directly violated commands set by Allah Himself. This shows that the commands given by the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) are identical to the commands of Allah Almighty, regardless of whether they are explicitly mentioned in the Qur'an. Verifying the attribution of these commands to the Prophet is necessary, but once the attribution is established, rejecting them is tantamount to rejecting the commands of Allah Almighty.
(The Friday sermon is an essential component of the Friday prayer): Second, the Friday sermon is an essential part of the Friday prayer. Neglecting or disregarding it is not permissible for any Muslim. For this sermon, the units of the Friday prayer are reduced from four to two, and the sermon takes the place of the other two units, being divided into two parts just like the two units of prayer. According to the Qur'anic text, the sermon is also 'remembrance of Allah' (dhikrullah), meaning the essence of both the sermon and the prayer is the same. The only difference is that during the prayer, the Imam and the congregation turn toward Allah in remembrance, whereas during the sermon, the Imam turns toward the people to remind them of Allah. Today, it is unfortunate that the actual Friday sermon is often reduced to a few formal supplications, while the Imams dedicate their oratorical efforts to long speeches delivered before the formal sermon. These speeches often contain little remembrance of Allah and instead focus on other unnecessary matters. Regrettably, listeners often doze off, and those who try to listen become fatigued by the length of the speech.
(An unfortunate situation): Many people deliberately arrive at the mosque only when the formal sermon begins to avoid these long speeches. Although this formal sermon is the original one, it has become purely symbolic. This is a very unfortunate situation. It is necessary to restore the true importance of the original Friday sermon. Imams should use the formal sermon itself to remind people of essential matters, keeping it comprehensive, brief, and wise so that people listen with interest and benefit. People should be urged to arrive on time to listen to the sermon, and they should be made aware of the consequences of negligence.
(The preferred practice for Muslims on Friday): Third, the Qur'anic style of expression indicates that the preferred practice for a Muslim on Friday is to spend the time before the prayer preparing for it. No other engagement should be introduced without an urgent need that might interfere with this preparation. This is derived from the verse stating: 'when the prayer has been concluded, disperse within the land and seek from the bounty of Allah'. These words imply that the appropriate time for business activities on Friday is after the prayer, not before it—especially those business activities that require people to disperse from the town, which might disrupt the unity required for the Friday gathering. A common person should have at least one day a week to groom themselves, wash clothes, and bathe, and Friday is the most suitable day for this as these are part of its etiquettes. Thus, Allah Almighty directed that other economic and commercial activities, if necessary, should begin after the Friday prayer. This was the way of our righteous predecessors, and it remains the preferred practice in circles where Islamic values and culture are kept alive.
Regarding Friday, we have restricted our discussion to the points directly derived from the Qur'an. We have not addressed other issues belonging to the domain of jurisprudence (Fiqh). With the grace and guidance of Allah Almighty, the commentary of this Surah is completed. All praise belongs to Allah for His favor. Lahore, March 25, 1978 / Rabi' al-Thani 14, 1398 AH.
